findings.summary: Layer boundary partially enforced but InteractiveShell leaks Application-layer dependency patterns into a Foundation-layer module
evidence: interactive_shell.py line 82-101: _ensure_discovery() is designed to avoid importing system_discovery (Application layer), but the do_explore, do_capabilities, do_forage methods all check 'if not self.discovery' and silently return or print limited-mode messages, meaning the module is architecturally unusable as a Foundation module — its primary commands require self.discovery which can only be set by injecting an Application-layer object.

interactive_shell.py line 254-256: do_capabilities calls self.discovery._discover_modules() directly, bypassing any abstraction layer and coupling to private implementation details of the discovery object.

git_operations/core/git.py line 8-14: imports PERFORMANCE_MONITOR_AVAILABLE from performance module, and conditionally imports monitor_performance at module level — this creates a hard conditional import that silently drops monitoring; the module-level if-guard means the import succeeds without error but the symbols are never in scope when psutil is absent, leading to NameError if misused.

agents/pai/trust_gateway.py line 36-42: imports from mcp_bridge and mcp.discovery at module top level, creating a tight coupling between trust_gateway and the bridge implementation; changes to the bridge directly affect the trust surface.
findings.suggestion: Separate the Foundation-layer shell infrastructure (Shell, InteractiveShell from shells/__init__.py) from the user-facing interactive exploration shell which genuinely depends on Application-layer discovery. The exploration shell belongs at the Application layer alongside system_discovery. This would also remove the silent no-op path in do_explore.

findings.summary: Inconsistent logging style: f-strings used in logger calls throughout git_operations and coding modules despite Python logging best practices discouraging eager interpolation
evidence: branching.py lines 18, 26, 38, 46, 71, 89, 93, 96: all logger calls use f-strings (e.g. logger.info(f"Creating new branch '{branch_name}' in {repository_path}")) instead of %-style lazy formatting.
merge.py lines 29-31, 46: logger.info(f"Merging branch '{source_branch}' into '{target_branch}'") — same f-string pattern.
reviewer.py line 147: logger.warning(f"Error loading config from {self.config_path}: {e}") uses f-string.
static_analyzer.py lines 155, 303: logger.warning and logger.error calls use f-strings.

By contrast, agents/pai/trust_gateway.py and collaboration/swarm/manager.py use the %-style format consistently (e.g. logger.info('Tool %s promoted to TRUSTED', tool_name)), creating a split convention across the codebase.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on %-style lazy formatting for all logger calls (logger.info('Creating branch %r in %s', branch_name, repository_path)). This avoids string construction overhead at log levels that are filtered out and is the recommended Python convention.

findings.summary: AgentProxy.send_task returns a hardcoded stub string in production code, violating the project's zero-mock policy
evidence: collaboration/protocols/swarm.py line 19-20: def send_task(self, task: str) -> str: return f'Result from {self.name}' — this is a hardcoded fake return value in production code.
The class docstring on line 12-13 says 'Mock-friendly proxy for a Codomyrmex agent (Legacy)' — describing it as mock-friendly explicitly contradicts the project's stated zero-mock/zero-stub policy.

The test in test_collaboration.py line 15-23 constructs SwarmManager with AgentProxy objects and asserts execute() returns a dict — this test exercises the stub path and passes because the legacy adapter never calls send_task at all, masking the dead stub.
findings.suggestion: Either raise NotImplementedError in send_task to comply with zero-mock policy, or remove AgentProxy entirely if the legacy compatibility layer is truly only used for test setup. Document the deprecation timeline per the project's stated policy.

findings.summary: Mixed error-handling contracts: some functions return False/None on failure while others raise; no consistent policy within git_operations
evidence: branching.py lines 9-27: create_branch raises subprocess.CalledProcessError on failure (docstring says Raises:), returning True only on success.
branching.py lines 74-97: get_current_branch catches CalledProcessError and returns None on failure — completely different contract from create_branch which raises.
branching.py lines 100-127: list_branches catches CalledProcessError and returns [] on failure — silent-return convention.
merge.py lines 49-56: merge_branch catches CalledProcessError and returns False — yet another convention within the same module group.
Callers cannot write uniform error-handling code because each function in the same module family uses a different failure signalling mechanism (raise, return None, return False, return []).
findings.suggestion: Pick one convention for git_operations/core/commands: either all functions raise on failure, or all return a Result-like object. The existing code in trust_gateway.py and collaboration/swarm/pool.py uses exceptions consistently (AssignmentError, SecurityError) and is a better model to follow.

findings.summary: Bare except-Exception in discovery.py and interactive_shell.py swallows all errors including KeyboardInterrupt subclasses and SystemExit
evidence: discovery.py lines 102-106: except Exception as exc followed by traceback.print_exc() and logger.warning — this is a legitimate broad catch but prints directly to stderr via traceback.print_exc(), mixing output channels.

discovery.py lines 134-135: for target in scan_targets: try: ... except Exception as e: logger.warning(f'Failed to scan package {target}: {e}') — swallows all errors silently, including potential programming errors (AttributeError, TypeError) that should propagate.

interactive_shell.py lines 366-367, 416-417, 433-434, 465-466: multiple _demo_*() methods each have bare except Exception: print(f'... demo failed: {e}') — these catch and print but do not log, inconsistent with the rest of the module which uses logger.
interactive_shell.py lines 826-830: run() method catches both KeyboardInterrupt and bare Exception but formats them as print() not logger calls.
findings.suggestion: Narrow except clauses to specific expected exception types. Use logger.exception() instead of traceback.print_exc() + logger.warning() to produce structured stack traces. Never use bare except in discovery scan loops without at minimum re-raising non-fatal-but-unexpected types.

findings.summary: TrustRegistry._load() is called redundantly before almost every mutating operation, creating implicit read-your-own-writes semantics that are unclear and potentially expensive
evidence: trust_gateway.py lines 366-368: verify_all_safe calls self._load() with comment 'Refresh first'.
trust_gateway.py lines 381-382: trust_tool calls self._load() with comment 'Refresh first'.
trust_gateway.py lines 395-397: trust_all calls self._load() with comment 'Refresh first'.
trust_gateway.py lines 418-421: get_report calls self._load() with comment 'Reload before reporting'.
trust_gateway.py lines 437-438: get_aggregate_level calls self._load().

trust_gateway.py line 361: even level() calls self._load() on every invocation — this means a call to trusted_call_tool (which calls level() and is_trusted()) triggers at minimum two disk reads per tool call.
There is no caching, TTL, or dirty-flag mechanism on the in-memory state, so disk I/O occurs on every read in a hot path.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a dirty-flag or file-modification-time check before unconditionally calling _load(). For in-process single-agent use, the ledger should only be re-read if the file's mtime has changed. Alternatively, document the multi-process assumption explicitly and accept the cost as intentional.

findings.summary: StaticAnalyzer and CodeReviewer duplicate identical _detect_language, _should_analyze_file, _generate_summary, and _check_tools_availability logic with no shared base
evidence: static_analyzer.py lines 160-181: _detect_language contains an identical language_map dict to reviewer.py lines 188-208.
static_analyzer.py lines 311-328: _should_analyze_file with identical supported_extensions set to reviewer.py lines 211-216.
reviewer.py lines 300-317 and static_analyzer.py lines 330-348: _generate_summary are structurally identical loops populating by_severity, by_category, by_rule from self.results.
reviewer.py lines 151-185 and static_analyzer.py lines 78-116: _check_tools_availability contain nearly identical subprocess.run(['tool', '--version']) probe loops with the same exception handling.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared AnalysisBase class or module-level helper functions (detect_language, should_analyze_file, probe_tools, generate_summary) in a common.py within the coding package. Both StaticAnalyzer and CodeReviewer should inherit or call these shared helpers.

findings.summary: discovery.py references undefined module-level variable _CACHE_TIMESTAMP, which will raise NameError at runtime when _tool_invalidate_cache is called with a specific module argument
evidence: discovery.py line 29-30: global _CACHE_TIMESTAMP; _CACHE_TIMESTAMP = 0.0 — this references a global name _CACHE_TIMESTAMP that is never declared at module scope.
The module declares _CACHE_EXPIRY at line 46 and _DYNAMIC_TOOLS_CACHE at line 42, but _CACHE_TIMESTAMP does not appear in any module-level assignment.
Calling _tool_invalidate_cache(module='some_module') will raise NameError: name '_CACHE_TIMESTAMP' is not defined at the global _CACHE_TIMESTAMP = 0.0 line.
The invalidate_tool_cache() path (called when module=None) works correctly because it uses _CACHE_EXPIRY instead, so this bug is only triggered on the module-specific invalidation branch.
findings.suggestion: Add _CACHE_TIMESTAMP: float = 0.0 at module level adjacent to the other cache state variables (lines 42-48), or replace the reference to _CACHE_TIMESTAMP with _CACHE_EXPIRY = 0.0 if the intent is to force a cache refresh by making the expiry in the past.

findings.summary: performance/__init__.py silently sets PerformanceMonitor=None when psutil is absent, but reviewer.py and static_analyzer.py then define their own duplicate no-op fallback classes rather than using the module flag
evidence: performance/__init__.py lines 36-52: defines PERFORMANCE_MONITOR_AVAILABLE and sets PerformanceMonitor=None when psutil is absent.

reviewer.py lines 39-72: defines its own local monitor_performance no-op decorator and performance_context no-op context manager when the import fails — duplicating the responsibility that belongs to performance/__init__.py.
static_analyzer.py lines 37-59: defines another copy of the same no-op monitor_performance and performance_context fallbacks.

The no-op performance_context in static_analyzer.py returns None from __init__ (line 51) which is harmless but semantically wrong — __init__ should return None always but the explicit 'return None' is misleading.
These three parallel fallback implementations will diverge over time since they are maintained independently.
findings.suggestion: Move the no-op fallback implementations into performance/__init__.py alongside PERFORMANCE_MONITOR_AVAILABLE, so all callers simply do 'from codomyrmex.performance import monitor_performance, performance_context' without any fallback logic in consumer modules.

findings.summary: Test quality is strong in swarm/test_swarm.py (class-based, behavioral, edge cases) but weak in test_collaboration.py (top-level functions, trivial assertions, stub proxy exercises no real behavior)
evidence: test_swarm.py: 8 test classes, 28+ test functions covering AgentPool role filtering, capability matching, load balancing, release, AssignmentError on no-available-agent, consensus weighted/veto/majority/deadlock, cyclic dependency detection, MessageBus wildcard routing, handler error isolation, and history — all behavioral.

test_collaboration.py lines 12-45: three top-level test functions (test_swarm_execution, test_task_decomposition, test_consensus) that exercise the legacy protocols/swarm.py shim, which itself delegates to the real swarm — these tests assert isinstance(results, dict) and len(tasks)==2 without checking actual behavior.

test_collaboration.py line 84-87: test_has_cycle_detects_cycle directly injects into private attributes (g._dependencies, g._dependents) to manufacture a cycle, bypassing the public API — this is a test isolation workaround that couples tests to private implementation.

test_trust_gateway_audit.py: well-structured class-based tests with concurrent-write safety test, TTL cleanup test, and proper use of tempfile for export — this is the strongest test file in the sample.
findings.suggestion: Promote the top-level functions in test_collaboration.py to class-based tests like the swarm tests. Remove direct _private attribute manipulation; instead add a public method or constructor parameter to DependencyGraph that allows injecting a pre-built graph for testing purposes.

findings.summary: Multiple files show structural patterns consistent with AI generation: over-commented obvious code, emoji-heavy UI strings embedded in business logic, generic docstrings, and duplicate fallback boilerplate across unrelated modules
evidence: interactive_shell.py lines 38-47: intro string embeds 10+ emojis (🐜, 🔍, etc.) and 'epistemic forager' metaphor inconsistently applied throughout; the foraging_messages list (lines 68-78) contains 8 thematically redundant strings that serve no functional distinction.

interactive_shell.py lines 305-326: do_demo method has a nested list of 4 random theatrical messages ('Ladies and gentlemen, the show is about to begin!') that add no value and would not be written by a developer aware of user experience.

reviewer.py and static_analyzer.py: both contain identical fallback boilerplate (monitor_performance no-op decorator, performance_context no-op context manager) suggesting copy-paste generation rather than shared infrastructure design.

reviewer.py lines 111-149: _load_config returns a hardcoded config dict with specific numeric defaults (max_complexity: 15, min_clone_similarity: 0.8, lsh_threshold: 500) that appear plausible but are undocumented as to their origin or tuning rationale.

collaboration/protocols/swarm.py: the AgentProxy.send_task stub, combined with the SwarmManager.add_agent shim translating legacy roles, has the characteristic shape of AI-generated compatibility scaffolding rather than intentional design.

generic one-liner docstrings appear in multiple places: reviewer.py lines 48-49 'def decorator(func): """Decorator."""', static_analyzer.py lines 41-42 same pattern — these docstrings convey no information.
findings.suggestion: Audit the interactive shell for value-neutral UI strings and remove or simplify them. Extract the shared fallback boilerplate into the performance module. Review all single-word docstrings ('Decorator.', 'Wrapper.') and replace with meaningful descriptions or remove them. The 'Legacy Compatibility' shim in protocols/swarm.py should have a documented deprecation date.

findings.summary: InteractiveShell class name collision: two unrelated classes named InteractiveShell exist in different modules with different interfaces
evidence: terminal_interface/shells/__init__.py lines 182-279: defines InteractiveShell as a subprocess-based session wrapper with start/send/read_output/execute/stop interface.
terminal_interface/shells/interactive_shell.py lines 29-836: defines InteractiveShell as a cmd.Cmd subclass providing a command loop for humans to explore the ecosystem.

These two classes have completely different constructors, interfaces, and purposes. Both are importable as 'from codomyrmex.terminal_interface.shells import InteractiveShell' (from __init__.py) or 'from codomyrmex.terminal_interface.shells.interactive_shell import InteractiveShell' — but a user importing from the package gets the subprocess session wrapper, not the exploration shell.

The exploration-shell class overrides do_clear (line 761-782) and resets session_data but introduces a type regression: 'discoveries_made' changes from list to set and 'demos_run' changes from int to set — breaking the type contract established in __init__.
findings.suggestion: Rename the cmd.Cmd-based exploration shell to ExplorationShell or EcosystemShell and document the distinction clearly. Fix the do_clear type regression where discoveries_made and demos_run types are changed from what __init__ established.

findings.summary: The three-tier trust model (UNTRUSTED->VERIFIED->TRUSTED) is well-conceived and consistently implemented, but the dual-tracking of trust level via both _trust_level global and TrustRegistry creates redundant state that can desynchronize
evidence: trust_gateway.py lines 64: _trust_level: TrustLevel = TrustLevel.UNTRUSTED — module-level global tracking global trust state.
trust_gateway.py lines 310-483: TrustRegistry._levels tracks per-tool trust levels, and get_aggregate_level() derives the global level from them.
trust_gateway.py lines 604-629: trust_tool() updates _registry per-tool AND then derives aggregate and updates _trust_level — two sources of truth that must be kept synchronized.

trust_gateway.py line 787-794: get_current_trust_level() ignores _trust_level entirely and derives from _registry.get_aggregate_level() — meaning the module-level _trust_level global is only authoritative for reset_trust().
The mismatch is acknowledged in comments (line 618-621) but the dual state is never fully resolved: _trust_level exists but is not the canonical source.
findings.suggestion: Remove the module-level _trust_level global and derive trust level solely from the registry's get_aggregate_level(). The only state that needs to be maintained is the per-tool registry. This eliminates the synchronization burden and makes the code easier to reason about.

findings.summary: get_tool_registry() rebuilds a full registry on every call with no caching, causing O(n) dynamic tool discovery scans on each invocation
evidence: server.py lines 35-68: get_tool_registry() creates a new _ToolRegistry(), registers all static tools, then calls _discover_dynamic_tools() which itself has a TTL cache — but each call to get_tool_registry() still iterates over all _TOOL_DEFINITIONS and all dynamic tools to populate a fresh registry object.
trust_gateway.py lines 269-280: _get_destructive_tools() calls get_tool_registry() which rebuilds the registry. _get_safe_tools() also calls get_tool_registry().

trust_gateway.py lines 304-306: module-level SAFE_TOOLS, SAFE_TOOL_COUNT, DESTRUCTIVE_TOOL_COUNT are eagerly computed at import time by calling _get_safe_tools() and _get_destructive_tools(), each rebuilding the registry — three separate registry builds at import.

trusted_call_tool() calls get_tool_registry() on line 683 (known_tools), then _registry.level() which calls _registry.level which calls nothing but does call self._load() — so a single tool call triggers at minimum one full registry rebuild.
findings.suggestion: Cache the output of get_tool_registry() with the same TTL as _DYNAMIC_TOOLS_CACHE, invalidating when invalidate_tool_cache() is called. The _ToolRegistry object can be a module-level singleton refreshed on cache invalidation.

findings.summary: Inconsistent None-handling for optional repository_path parameter across git_operations functions — some use 'if x is None: x = os.getcwd()' while others use 'cwd = x or "."'
evidence: branching.py lines 9-16: create_branch uses 'if repository_path is None: repository_path = os.getcwd()' pattern.
branching.py lines 29-36: switch_branch uses same pattern.

branching.py lines 100-111: list_branches uses 'cwd = repository_path or "."' — this differs from the previous pattern in two ways: it uses a dot string as fallback instead of os.getcwd(), and it uses truthiness test (or) rather than None identity (is None), meaning an empty-string repository_path would fall through to '.' rather than raising.

The 'or "."' pattern is subtly different from os.getcwd(): a '.' relative path is interpreted relative to the process CWD, which is the same in practice but different in semantics and different from what the docstring implies ('Defaults to current directory').
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a single pattern for all git command functions. Prefer 'if repository_path is None: repository_path = os.getcwd()' since it is explicit about None-checking, returns an absolute path, and matches the pattern used in the rest of the module.

findings.summary: do_clear() in interactive_shell.py resets session_data with mismatched types relative to __init__, silently introducing a bug where subsequent session stats will fail or produce wrong output
evidence: interactive_shell.py lines 61-66: __init__ sets session_data with 'commands_run': 0 (int), 'modules_explored': set() (set), 'discoveries_made': [] (list), 'demos_run': 0 (int).

interactive_shell.py lines 776-782: do_clear resets session_data with 'commands_run': 0 (correct), 'modules_explored': set() (correct), 'discoveries_made': set() (WRONG — was list), 'demos_run': set() (WRONG — was int).

After do_clear, do_session at line 669 iterates over session_data['discoveries_made'] — this works whether it is list or set, but line 662 prints len(discoveries_made) which also works — the bug surfaces only if code appends to discoveries_made after clear (append works on list, not set).
interactive_shell.py line 783: self.command_history = [] sets an attribute that was never initialized in __init__, creating an inconsistent object state.
findings.suggestion: Extract a _reset_session_data() private method called from both __init__ and do_clear to guarantee type consistency. Decide whether discoveries_made is a list or set and use that type exclusively.

findings.summary: Legacy compatibility shim in collaboration/protocols/swarm.py inherits from the new SwarmManager while overriding methods with incompatible return types, creating a Liskov Substitution Principle violation
evidence: protocols/swarm.py line 22: class SwarmManager(NewSwarmManager) — inherits from the real SwarmManager.

protocols/swarm.py lines 35-46: execute() method returns dict[str, str] (task_id to description), whereas the parent's execute_mission() is async and returns list[dict[str, Any]] — completely incompatible return types.

protocols/swarm.py lines 48-62: consensus_vote() calls asyncio.run() inside the method, meaning calling it from an already-running event loop (common in async contexts) will raise RuntimeError: This event loop is already running. The try/except RuntimeError creates a new loop — this pattern is fragile and can leak resources.
protocols/swarm.py line 64-72: TaskDecomposer(NewTaskDecomposer) overrides decompose() to return list[str] while the parent returns list[SubTask] — different return types on the same method name.
The legacy shim is imported and used in tests (test_collaboration.py line 4-8) and tests pass — meaning the type divergences are never caught by existing tests.
findings.suggestion: Do not use inheritance for legacy compatibility when return types are incompatible. Use composition or a standalone adapter class that wraps but does not extend the new SwarmManager. Alternatively, mark the entire protocols/swarm.py module with a clear deprecation date and prevent new code from importing it.
